speaker: interviewer
text: Henry Silver. Mr. Silver, tell us some more about your experience in the ghetto, how you helped yourself and your family to manage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- I was young in this time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents was middle age. The-- the people all the middle age, been sent away like I mentioned before. I hate to repeat myself again. Let's see, it was 35,000 Jews. Let's see, altogether was going and let's say the half was left, but 18,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: And during this time my parents been taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see-- did you see people being taken to the train station or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they know its going to happen. They, let's say they put up big reflectors, bit lights. They know it's going to happen today, and they take all of them, everybody aroused, everybody outside to the apartment to the living quarters. In-- in this section it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] few German dignitaries, like big officers, Hauptscharfuhrer. They look and they didn't like, you left. They didn't like you. Why I'm telling you this, because between them my parents haven't been young enough. They send them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw them being sent away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw later on my own from my brothers, it was youngster. They took the youngsters, the people who didn't like how you walk, didn't like the look, they didn't like. They-- they killed them in place. My brother, they took some younger people to bury them. It's after this everything, everything went back to-- to-- in a way, to normal. My brother was-- he was born in 1920-- it was 1925. It was at least quite a few younger than me. He claimed to me this. He buried, he saw between the dead people, between the older and the people what, they couldn't run fast. They couldn't walk fast. They didn't be liked, but its-- this time was already SS people or German people. This he buried them. He recognized them between the dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this time I went, still we got a rabbi. I went to this rabbi and ask him a question. You think I didn't see them dead? According to Jews, you dead, you say a prayer, like Kaddish. Then he say, no, you didn't see this. Your brother told you. Is-- Were you living in the ghetto with your family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still in the office?

words.text: You were still in the office?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been living in this over-- over where we had this place of business. I was-- you can call this between the pretend this was you have been middle class or you could afford it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many-- most of the time, most of the time I got places where you could even in camp we hiding. We was hiding. Everything. I find a German and good German like the gentleman say was good people, he say [GERMAN]. You would still help a German, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, you explain it.

speaker: interviewee
text: A good Jew is-- is in hiding. But he is not touchable is the best Jude. A good Jude, a live Jude is-- he's in hiding. That's been best Jude verstreck Jude.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now how long were you hiding in this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, hiding, you hide were only for a few hours. What you mean, you couldn't hide? I haven't been hidden like-- like-- like a lot of people. My brother was survived, he got like this guy say, a Gentile lady, a good friend of his. We in [INAUDIBLE] money. She made the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like the grave. She made them dig the hole, and then she put them in holes. She hide them during the-- they felt this is-- they come, they're going to look-- look for Jews, look, is he went this hole, gutter. She was hiding me. And he survived the war in hiding. Even half the time he was in-- in a concentration camp, not a concentration, Arbeitslager, be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but when you were there, you would just hide for a few hours. How did you know when to hide and when to come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know? What-- how did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question.  I don't know how to tell you how I knew. You-- you got that instinct. This is the reason people would survive. They got a-- a-- a feeling to survive. They know, it came for same-- same thing, you learn how to survive. You would learn how to survive. You would, like I-- I'm going to tell you later on, I survived with-- in the biggest worst concentration. I trade liquor, liquor. I trade, [NON-ENGLISH]. Give me a taste, you know. And an SS woman give me this. Was a reason she likes me. I was dressed my-- with the help from Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll learn about that in a minute. When you were hiding from time to time when you were still in Radom and in the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how did you, let's see, you knew there was going to be a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure you know.  You got the feeling through different ways. You-- you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you watch any of the selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been in the selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, describe to me a selection you were in, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say you-- I got to tell you, I've been the first selection, but I survived this is with my wife. It's always you got to have people to where you can-- that you can ask him to help you. I had friend, afraid, but he been a-- a policeman. He been a friend. He been a friend of my cousin, whatever. Is the-- he-- he know the Sturmbannfuhrer. He know the-- the German when I passed by. First thing he turned and document. He say, he showed them and says, he's my friend. And he saved me. He saved me and Edith. It was a lot of incidents like this, thousands. Find there'd been a different way how to-- to save his miserable life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you started all this in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already later, you asked me for later in ghetto. In '39, I told you already--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first ghetto was right, like I told you, was 35,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I know. But what I want to know is when did-- did you ever move to the ghetto where you had to live there yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After few weeks or months to fighting to bribes to get me out in the city where you lived together with-- everybody lived together, Poles, Jews, whatever. Is-- is they made it official to-- they let you know this people from this street and this street and this street have to move to this particular place. They didn't told you it was a ghetto that you have to move there. We know already, because later on the separate day, they put walls.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's get you from the office to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was that in a few months or what? How long-- how long were you just staying, living in this office and hiding from time to time, and then when you-- and then how long would it be before you had to go to live in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I told you already. I lived already in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: By one year? By one month? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how long? I would say about a year, let's say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by this time were your parents and your family gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're gone already.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're gone. So you're by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by myself. I had a brother living in the ghetto and other place. And I had a sister when she was with Polish friends. She lived over with Polish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was good Polish people, good. They got their reason, I would say very, very few was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith I met before the war. When I-- I was in this youth movement. Edith was in this youth movement. She was younger than me is, a big difference, seven years. I was [NON-ENGLISH], a leader within her group.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I get married, it was a crazy, a funny time. The first time, I was good off during the war, during this year, but I could be associated with other people. Is-- is I got married. It was crazy. They took away the parent. They took away friends. They took away and so many thousand, thousand, you get smaller and smaller. Everybody in this time, I know Edith for years. And I told her when she feels really brought that closer. Incident, I got to tell you a little story. it brought us closer together in this time because she was seven years younger. She was only 19, 20 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is that incident, you know, if the-- the German need help, they didn't go in to organization ask for help where they hired it. They just wen in the street and they look up for Jews. They recognize you, if not the Pole, then this is Jude, this is a Jude. It's they-- they never would catch me. I was, I think I was very took chances or I was not a coward. Like I'd say whatever is going to happen. I was not afraid what was going to happen. You die, you die especially you learn in the war. If they look for-- for help workers, they need let's see 20, 20 people to work, they catch, they catch, then they pick them up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Is that it so happen I wear a-- a leather jacket, and the looking of this leather jacket, I ran away in the SS. Two SS guys run after me. And I say, no, they're not going to catch me, or this-- they saw me from far away with this leather jacket. Finally I couldn't fight. I ran up a place in-- in a case like this, if you can run and they could not let you in was Jewish people was smaller ghetto. They're afraid to let you in. If they let you in, you know, they're in trouble. They couldn't help it. They caught me. Edith saw me that the-- the two SS run after me. They bro-- they're so happy, I remember now like a Saturday. A Saturday night, they brought me through many difference places where, SS, they brought me to a place, the German gendarmes. And he-- this gendarmes that recognized me, I sold him something. He recognized me in this time. He-- he told me I should pay him 50 zlotys, and he'd let me free accidentally. I was lucky this time. And he let me free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this time I got more closer to Edith, and we kept very closer together. And everybody say you don't know what's going to happen tomorrow or next day. And let's get-- get married. How long we get the rabbi left? And we've been-- What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I think '41, must be after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived somehow between '39 and '41 by buying and selling and trading a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trading a lot, if not--

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't live today.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Poles, with Germans with who?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Poles, with everybody where you could do a normal business. Is you always, when you moved in your own place, you try to bring enough fruitful merchandise that you can trade, you could trade for-- for food, for whatever. Is you brought, and this is not an easy job to bring this lot of stuff to the ghetto. How you could do this, you've got to have place. You have to find that place and the way to stock the stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with? Were you with some other people that you worked with to help you and you helped them, or how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: A funny thing, later on after '41, we got married, it was only with Edith. I-- she--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? Edith what?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Eda, ?] Edith. Similar. Edith is her Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her last-- what was her last name before you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neuman. As matter of fact, she looks very much like not like Jewish personality, like Gentile. So I sent her to places where I used to do business. And she's done [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] exceptional good. We done everything together later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a team.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a team.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Edith became a team.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And did she help with-- she helped with the buying and the selling and managing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did Edith get pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still-- she got-- you ask a good question. Everything is a story. After we got married, we try not to get-- she tried very hard not to get pregnant. You know what happened, she got pregnant. And we still got doctors, which you can make abort the child and the-- her mother was still alive. And she was after her, and don't do this. Young, you understand a little bit Jewish? Not even Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what that means? You don't cut a young tree, something like that she tell you. Please don't do it, and-- and I come this story how he survived when he was 9 month old. Let's-- let's-- before he's nine months old, how did she manage during her pregnancy? How did you help her get enough food.

speaker: interviewee
text: She done everything she-- to abort the child or still how she done. She done a lot to help, like I told you this, youth moment, lot was nurses and lot. She got help. We paid them some of the Poles used to come to the small ghetto pick up, or I opened a place to hide to be able to get out in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Polish business and everything was less and less. So I still, I accumulated so much, even when couldn't do it. The-- the German would took care of this what she took away from the Jews. He said in German, the Jude must be-- you don't understand German? The Jude is a verrucht Jude. How's a-- he's a a crazy Jew. Why he accumulated so much junk? And how did you keep all this merchandise? Where did you have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I keep telling you, you-- you learn to bribe, to get places where you can hold the stuff, in special-- then you move. And people was glad to help moving because you paid him. You-- you learn like I survive. You couldn't go like this. You are dead pigeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain what you meant by--

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to go push yourself like this, not to let like that this. You know what that mean? Not to let the hand down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to keep thinking and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep figuring it out and keep being forceful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And figure it out, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now what? Is it 1940-- 41--

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41 or '40-- '41, how with this child, how they survived--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she have the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] listen, help me. This is a good story. It just how it's possible. Human mind work, how is it possible to organize was seven children left and was left there and small real-- they don't call this a ghetto, call this a small Arbeitslager, a small place. They still got a little hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living in this Arbeitslager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was living with Edith.

speaker: interviewer
text: With Edith in this Arbeitslager.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, small, in this little ghetto, but the German called this the Arbeitslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a better place than some other people had? A little safer, a little better.

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask a good question. You, everything if you know how to organize, to bribe, to find yourself, to know the city where the place you went. Even was a place later on what a better place. Edith was mad at me. Take a look, we could get over there. Then you know the area where the-- they-- they let us know, this street, this street, this area, this area is going to be belong to this ghettos. You know. You organize. You try to find it, a better living place where you can live. If  you didn't know to go, you haven't got nothing, you going like this. You--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a network.

speaker: interviewee
text: You died in ghetto. You cramped, lice, and the dirt eat you up. If you know how to-- to help yourself. Is you-- if not, I wouldn't be alive. Edith wouldn't be alive if you wouldn't no evening camp. Yeah. Yeah. It's later one--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So you're in this Arbeitslager and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And on day, they can, the German came and they say, they have to make Radom Juden free. You understand this, what it means. They have to-- no, no more Jude there. All of them. You have-- what you have to do with them, they-- they give a place they call-- I bet your interview, did they ever mentioned the name, Szkolna. They took-- you interview people where they mention Szkolna? This was, they made it a [NON-ENGLISH]. To make-- to make a place like this they took-- I always, if you have to do something, you raise the hand, you know how to do it. You know, even you going to do something different, you-- you-- you know how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in a selection that you would raise your hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I raised the hand, no, this is lot of places. The German come down this, they need-- they need 200 people to build-- to build barracks. We know already for another two Germans to-- to do is I went to this-- to this-- I'd be able to do this. It so happened, having went through there is the even the last minute, they make-- they make this small place judenrein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I through a selection where I finally, I ran away. I don't remember exactly was, I got myself in in this-- this new Lager, but I build it. It's I know how to go around, what to do, because I helped build the barracks, the-- the living quarter with everything what is need.

speaker: interviewer
text: You physically built it with other men working?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the other men, let's say 100 people, 150 people, whatever. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know the layout of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know the layout, and Edith always you've got half a [NON-ENGLISH], something to show. You-- you work out with special where you work for Germans or you work for productive place. And she was working, they organize a shop but worked for-- for-- for German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do in the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: She learned how to be-- to sewing. She's a good dressmaker. But she learned in camp. She learned. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She worked about two, three months before in this place for the Germans. She got in touch with German to measure their whatever. And when they make the list, I have to go back and tell you how you ask me a question. How seven, eight children or whatever could live 9 year-- 9 months old, 10 months. We organized a [NON-ENGLISH], like an orphanage. We find that everything, if you know how to go where I like. You go around hide too. We have possibility how to organize. Got in touch with the doctor with us left, and he got a nurse, a German nurse. During the day, she took care of the-- of the children. And when the last many-- then you couldn't hide the less-- the some guys were so smart and so capable to doing what I was capable to do. Even they brought the-- the-- the rest of the small Arbeitslager they brought them to the judenrein. They brought them to the Szkolna. They liquidate even this-- this shop where Edith used to take later on the kids for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Edith would take the kids to work and hide them there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And hide them. And what we done when they always less and less and less. They liquidate the-- the weaker one, or less productive one. There was a-- a small-- a small amount left in we-- is we-- I was in the Szkolna building this-- this camp over there. She was-- is I find that us to what I tell you, the Besten Jude vestector We called them [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a very good German. I saw what-- we know what's going on. He know already couple weeks before, because we built for them, what is going to go now with the-- with the people. We know the few children was over there. You couldn't say me what a [NON-ENGLISH]. Is tomorrow is going to be this and this and this and this. And he told me the best Jude is a versteck Jude. That's what I tell, the best safe Jew is a-- a Jew what is in hiding. That's what he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you don't know, I gave name from German. He worked in an office, in the office with this Judenlager. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What we done, you say during-- during this-- during this-- this time, we-- we exchange this people to this Szkolna. My son was between them, and he told me this. He said, I let them know, we still haven't got the place, a kitchen. They brought food from the small Arbeitslager small ghetto to this Szkolna. I got somehow in touch with Edith. She should-- she should take the child, put in a rucksack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When she's going to come, we know when she's going to come with the child. She is dead. The child is dead. They don't build for children an orphanage home. And she done, what she should give him sleeping pills, and put him in a rucksack.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]
